title: Trump’s plan if the Supreme Court blocks tariffs

transcript.text: The Supreme Court is about to rule on
the the the the legal kind of
foundations of the the biggest tariff
package you have is the declaration that
there's an international economic
emergency underway. If they rule against
your government and say that that legal
base doesn't work, you have a plan B.
>> Yes.
>> If they say you have to refund all of
the tariff revenues you've collected,
that's like a hundred billion dollars,
something like that. Is that right?
>> That's about right.
>> How how does that work?
>> There are precedents for this. If you
look back, you know, we had some
litigation in the United States over
what's called a harbor maintenance fee
in the United States and our court of
international trade. The court itself
kind of helped administer that program.
So, I expect if if if there's, you know,
if the worst case scenario happens and
the courts, you know, uh overturn what's
happening, the courts will also have
some say in in how that might work, how
that process might work. And if
businesses are panicking that that the
replacement, the plan B will be just
like a tangle of a million different
bits of tariff legislation, the
complexity, that's a big big worry for
business. That's that's a reasonable
worry. No.
>> Yeah. So, I can certainly understand why
they would worry about that. I think our
goal is if we have to find a way to set
up uh a replacement system uh would be
to have as much continuity as possible.
You know, I mentioned before that there
are certain things that are subject to
tariffs, certain things that aren't. Our
goal would be to preserve that as much
as possible. Um I you know we have
agreements in place with a lot of
countries where they've agreed to open
their markets and they've agreed that we
can have a certain tariff level against
them. Uh all of those agreements stay in
place, right? Just because the Supreme
Court
>> but it's going to be more complicated,
right?
>> Uh yeah, it's inherently more
complicated. I mean the president chose
to use because it gives the flexibility.
It's a single tool and we can use it. Uh
the other thing to keep in mind is some
of the other tariff authorities that
have been quite important over the past
year include section 232 tariffs and
section 301 tariffs. They're not an
issue in this litigation and so that
will all stay uh in play.
